narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic records, test scores, and background details like family income. The model estimated a 63% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed right away to maintain their strong bar passage rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model weaker performance in later undergraduate years compared to peers. Next in importance, your LSAT score of 35 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's close to the average of 36.7 and shows solid standardized testing ability. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also nudged the odds toward failure, as it's below the average 6th decile, suggesting an early academic pattern that the model views as a concern despite your full-time student status, which is the norm for most applicants. Overall, these rankings outweighed positives like your undergraduate GPA of 3.1—near the 3.21 average—and your low family income bracket, which didn't strongly sway the outcome either way. Compared to typical students who get approved, your mid-range LSAT helped, but the lower deciles in both early and late undergrad years stood out as bigger risks for bar struggles. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male from a modest background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted effort—but the model focuses on patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. You might also explore schools with more holistic reviews or bar prep support tailored to full-time students like you. With focused improvements, you can reshape this prediction and build a stronger case for admission next cycle. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
